=== PLAN ===
# MLPL Autograd v1 Saga (v0.5)

## Goal

Make differentiation a language primitive. Land a reverse-mode
autograd engine on the existing `DenseArray` substrate, expose
`tensor[...]`, `param[...]`, and `grad` at the language level,
and port the v0.4 demos that currently hand-write their
gradients onto the new engine. No new backends, no optimizers
beyond SGD, no model DSL -- those are later sagas.

## Quality requirements (every step)

1. TDD: failing tests first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test` (all tests)
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-array` DenseArray, shape, reductions, matmul
- `mlpl-runtime` built-ins (softmax, argmax, blobs, random, ...)
- `mlpl-eval` tree-walking evaluator
- `mlpl-parser` with current surface syntax
- v0.4 demos: `tiny_mlp.mlpl`, `softmax_classifier.mlpl`, ...
  (both hand-rolling gradient formulas)

## Phases

### Phase 1: Autograd core (Rust)
- New crate `mlpl-autograd` with a reverse-mode tape.
- `Tensor` wrapper holding `{ value: DenseArray, grad: RefCell<Option<DenseArray>>, node: NodeId }`.
- Graph nodes for: add, sub, mul, div, neg, matmul, sum,
  mean, exp, log, relu, tanh, sigmoid, softmax, transpose,
  broadcast, reshape.
- `backward(root)` walks the tape in reverse topo order and
  accumulates gradients into leaf tensors marked `requires_grad`.
- Tests: gradcheck against finite differences for every op.

### Phase 2: Language surface
- Parser support for `param[shape]` and `tensor[shape]`
  constructors (trainable vs. non-trainable leaves).
- New built-in `grad(expr, wrt)` where `wrt` is a parameter or
  list of parameters; returns array(s) matching their shape.
- Evaluator wires array-valued operations through the autograd
  tape when any input is a tracked tensor.
- Tests: end-to-end `grad` calls on scalar and vector losses.

### Phase 3: Demo ports
- Rewrite `demos/tiny_mlp.mlpl` to use `param` + `grad` instead
  of hand-written backprop. Must still pass the existing
  "MLP beats linear on XOR" integration test.
- Rewrite `demos/softmax_classifier.mlpl` the same way. Must
  still reach > 95% accuracy on separable blobs.
- Keep SGD manual for now (`W <- W - lr * grad`).

### Phase 4: Tutorial, docs, release
- Add tutorial lesson "Automatic differentiation" walking from
  a scalar example to the MLP port.
- Update `docs/milestone-ml.md` (or new `milestone-autograd.md`),
  `docs/saga.md`, `docs/status.md`, `docs/plan.md`.
- Bump REPL banner to v0.5.
- Rebuild and deploy `pages/`.
- Tag `v0.5.0-autograd`.

## Success criteria

- `mlpl-autograd` crate exists with gradcheck-verified ops.
- `grad` built-in works in the REPL for scalar and vector losses.
- Tiny MLP and softmax classifier demos pass their existing
  accuracy tests with zero hand-written gradient math.
- Tutorial lesson on autograd renders in the web REPL.
- All quality gates green, pages deployed, release tagged.

content.content: 1	# Tiny 1-hidden-layer MLP on an XOR-style non-linearly separable dataset.

file.content: # Tiny 1-hidden-layer MLP on an XOR-style non-linearly separable dataset.

input.content:
# Tiny 1-hidden-layer MLP on an XOR-style non-linearly separable dataset.
#
# This version uses MLPL's autograd: parameters are declared with
# `param[...]` and gradients are computed with the `grad` built-in.
# SGD updates stay manual (W <- W - lr * grad).
#
# A linear softmax classifier (also autograd-powered) is trained on
# the same data for comparison: the MLP learns a curved boundary,
# the linear model cannot.

# ----- data: 4 blobs arranged as XOR, labeled by diagonal -----
D   = blobs(3, 20, [[-2,-2],[2,2],[-2,2],[2,-2]])
X   = matmul(D, [[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]])
raw = reshape(matmul(D, [[0],[0],[1]]), [80])
y   = gt(raw, 1.5)
Y   = one_hot(y, 2)

# Precomputed broadcast helper: grad() can't call `ones` directly,
# so we lift it into an environment variable used as a const leaf
# inside the loss expression.
O80 = ones([80, 1])

# ----- linear softmax baseline (autograd + MSE-on-one-hot loss) -----
# Biases have shape [1, C] so they can be broadcast via matmul(O80, b).
Wl = param[2, 2]
bl = param[1, 2]
lr = 0.3
repeat 300 {
  Wl = Wl - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y)), Wl);
  bl = bl - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y)), bl)
}
logits      = matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)
Pl          = softmax(logits, 1)
linear_pred = argmax(Pl, 1)
linear_accuracy = mean(eq(linear_pred, y))
linear_accuracy
confusion_matrix(linear_pred, y)

# ----- tiny MLP: 2 -> 8 -> 2 with tanh + softmax -----
# Declare trainable parameters, then reseed with random values. The
# reassignment keeps the `param` mark so `grad` still tracks them.
W1 = param[2, 8]
b1 = param[1, 8]
W2 = param[8, 2]
b2 = param[1, 2]
W1 = randn(5, [2, 8]) * 0.5
W2 = randn(6, [8, 2]) * 0.5
lr = 0.2

# Training loop with loss-curve capture: 20 outer steps of 30 updates
# each. `counter` indexes into a length-20 losses vector via an
# iota/eq mask (MLPL has no array indexing, so we broadcast instead).
# Each grad() call re-evaluates the MSE loss on the current parameters.
counter = 0
losses  = zeros([20])
repeat 20 {
  repeat 30 {
    W1 = W1 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), W1);
    b1 = b1 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), b1);
    W2 = W2 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), W2);
    b2 = b2 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), b2)
  };
  Z1 = matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1);
  H  = tanh_fn(Z1);
  Z2 = matmul(H, W2) + matmul(O80, b2);
  P  = softmax(Z2, 1);
  loss = 0 - mean(reduce_add(Y * log(P + 0.0001), 1));
  mask = eq(iota(20), counter);
  losses = losses + loss * mask;
  counter = counter + 1
}

# ----- evaluation -----
Z1 = matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)
H  = tanh_fn(Z1)
Z2 = matmul(H, W2) + matmul(O80, b2)
P  = softmax(Z2, 1)
mlp_pred = argmax(P, 1)
mlp_accuracy = mean(eq(mlp_pred, y))
mlp_accuracy

loss_curve(losses)

# Tiny 1-hidden-layer MLP on an XOR-style non-linearly separable dataset.
#
# This version uses MLPL's autograd: parameters are declared with
# `param[...]` and gradients are computed with the `grad` built-in.
# SGD updates stay manual (W <- W - lr * grad).
#
# A linear softmax classifier (also autograd-powered) is trained on
# the same data for comparison: the MLP learns a curved boundary,
# the linear model cannot.

# ----- data: 4 blobs arranged as XOR, labeled by diagonal -----
D   = blobs(3, 20, [[-2,-2],[2,2],[-2,2],[2,-2]])
X   = matmul(D, [[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]])
raw = reshape(matmul(D, [[0],[0],[1]]), [80])
y   = gt(raw, 1.5)
Y   = one_hot(y, 2)

# Precomputed broadcast helper: grad() can't call `ones` directly,
# so we lift it into an environment variable used as a const leaf
# inside the loss expression.
O80 = ones([80, 1])

# ----- linear softmax baseline (autograd + MSE-on-one-hot loss) -----
# Biases have shape [1, C] so they can be broadcast via matmul(O80, b).
Wl = param[2, 2]
bl = param[1, 2]
lr = 0.3
repeat 300 {
  Wl = Wl - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y)), Wl);
  bl = bl - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)) - Y)), bl)
}
logits      = matmul(X, Wl) + matmul(O80, bl)
Pl          = softmax(logits, 1)
linear_pred = argmax(Pl, 1)
linear_accuracy = mean(eq(linear_pred, y))
linear_accuracy
confusion_matrix(linear_pred, y)

# ----- tiny MLP: 2 -> 8 -> 2 with tanh + softmax -----
# Declare trainable parameters, then reseed with random values. The
# reassignment keeps the `param` mark so `grad` still tracks them.
W1 = param[2, 8]
b1 = param[1, 8]
W2 = param[8, 2]
b2 = param[1, 2]
W1 = randn(5, [2, 8]) * 0.5
W2 = randn(6, [8, 2]) * 0.5
lr = 0.2

# Training loop with loss-curve capture: 20 outer steps of 30 updates
# each. `counter` indexes into a length-20 losses vector via an
# iota/eq mask (MLPL has no array indexing, so we broadcast instead).
# Each grad() call re-evaluates the MSE loss on the current parameters.
counter = 0
losses  = zeros([20])
repeat 20 {
  repeat 30 {
    W1 = W1 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), W1);
    b1 = b1 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), b1);
    W2 = W2 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), W2);
    b2 = b2 - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(tanh(matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)), W2) + matmul(O80, b2)) - Y)), b2)
  };
  Z1 = matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1);
  H  = tanh_fn(Z1);
  Z2 = matmul(H, W2) + matmul(O80, b2);
  P  = softmax(Z2, 1);
  loss = 0 - mean(reduce_add(Y * log(P + 0.0001), 1));
  mask = eq(iota(20), counter);
  losses = losses + loss * mask;
  counter = counter + 1
}

# ----- evaluation -----
Z1 = matmul(X, W1) + matmul(O80, b1)
H  = tanh_fn(Z1)
Z2 = matmul(H, W2) + matmul(O80, b2)
P  = softmax(Z2, 1)
mlp_pred = argmax(P, 1)
mlp_accuracy = mean(eq(mlp_pred, y))
mlp_accuracy

loss_curve(losses)
confusion_matrix(mlp_pred, y)

# ----- MLP decision surface on a 30x30 grid -----
G   = grid([-4, 4, -4, 4], 30)
O900 = ones([900, 1])
GZ1 = matmul(G, W1) + matmul(O900, b1)
GH  = tanh_fn(GZ1)
GZ2 = matmul(GH, W2) + matmul(O900, b2)
GP  = softmax(GZ2, 1)
# Class-1 probability as the surface.
p1  = reshape(matmul(GP, [[0],[1]]), [900])
boundary_2d(p1, [30, 30], X, y)

# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```
/mw-cp

=== PLAN ===
# MLPL Autograd v1 Saga (v0.5)

## Goal

Make differentiation a language primitive. Land a reverse-mode
autograd engine on the existing `DenseArray` substrate, expose
`tensor[...]`, `param[...]`, and `grad` at the language level,
and port the v0.4 demos that currently hand-write their
gradients onto the new engine. No new backends, no optimizers
beyond SGD, no model DSL -- those are later sagas.

## Quality requirements (every step)

1. TDD: failing tests first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test` (all tests)
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-array` DenseArray, shape, reductions, matmul
- `mlpl-runtime` built-ins (softmax, argmax, blobs, random, ...)
- `mlpl-eval` tree-walking evaluator
- `mlpl-parser` with current surface syntax
- v0.4 demos: `tiny_mlp.mlpl`, `softmax_classifier.mlpl`, ...
  (both hand-rolling gradient formulas)

## Phases

### Phase 1: Autograd core (Rust)
- New crate `mlpl-autograd` with a reverse-mode tape.
- `Tensor` wrapper holding `{ value: DenseArray, grad: RefCell<Option<DenseArray>>, node: NodeId }`.
- Graph nodes for: add, sub, mul, div, neg, matmul, sum,
  mean, exp, log, relu, tanh, sigmoid, softmax, transpose,
  broadcast, reshape.
- `backward(root)` walks the tape in reverse topo order and
  accumulates gradients into leaf tensors marked `requires_grad`.
- Tests: gradcheck against finite differences for every op.

### Phase 2: Language surface
- Parser support for `param[shape]` and `tensor[shape]`
  constructors (trainable vs. non-trainable leaves).
- New built-in `grad(expr, wrt)` where `wrt` is a parameter or
  list of parameters; returns array(s) matching their shape.
- Evaluator wires array-valued operations through the autograd
  tape when any input is a tracked tensor.
- Tests: end-to-end `grad` calls on scalar and vector losses.

### Phase 3: Demo ports
- Rewrite `demos/tiny_mlp.mlpl` to use `param` + `grad` instead
  of hand-written backprop. Must still pass the existing
  "MLP beats linear on XOR" integration test.
- Rewrite `demos/softmax_classifier.mlpl` the same way. Must
  still reach > 95% accuracy on separable blobs.
- Keep SGD manual for now (`W <- W - lr * grad`).

### Phase 4: Tutorial, docs, release

# Linear softmax classifier on a 3-class gaussian-blobs dataset.
#
# Trains W (2x3) and b (length 3) by gradient descent on softmax
# cross-entropy, then renders:
#   - confusion matrix of predicted vs true labels
#   - the class-0 decision boundary on a 2D grid

# ----- data -----
D  = blobs(11, 30, [[0, 0], [4, 4], [-4, 4]])
X  = matmul(D, [[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]])
tl = reshape(matmul(D, [[0],[0],[1]]), [90])
Y  = one_hot(tl, 3)

# ----- parameters -----
W  = zeros([2, 3])
b  = zeros([3])
lr = 0.2

# ----- training (softmax cross-entropy gradient: (P - Y)) -----
repeat 300 {
  logits = matmul(X, W) + matmul(ones([90, 1]), reshape(b, [1, 3]));

  gW = matmul(transpose(X), dZ) / 90;
  gb = reduce_add(dZ, 0) / 90;
  W  = W - lr * gW;
  b  = b - lr * gb
}

# ----- evaluation -----
logits = matmul(X, W) + matmul(ones([90, 1]), reshape(b, [1, 3]))
P      = softmax(logits, 1)
pred   = argmax(P, 1)
accuracy = mean(eq(pred, tl))
accuracy

confusion_matrix(pred, tl)

# Evaluate the classifier on a 30x30 grid spanning the data range, then
# render the class-0 probability as the surface with the original
# points overlaid (as [x, y, label] via the stored blobs matrix).
G   = grid([-6, 6, -3, 6], 30)
gl  = matmul(G, W) + matmul(ones([900, 1]), reshape(b, [1, 3]))
gP  = softmax(gl, 1)
# Extract class-0 probability via matmul projection.
p0  = reshape(matmul(gP, [[1],[0],[0]]), [900])
boundary_2d(p0, [30, 30], X, tl)

## Goal

Make differentiation a language primitive. Land a reverse-mode
autograd engine on the existing `DenseArray` substrate, expose
`tensor[...]`, `param[...]`, and `grad` at the language level,
and port the v0.4 demos that currently hand-write their
gradients onto the new engine. No new backends, no optimizers
beyond SGD, no model DSL -- those are later sagas.

2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:

  mean, exp, log, relu, tanh, sigmoid, softmax, transpose,
  broadcast, reshape.

  accumulates gradients into leaf tensors marked `requires_grad`.
- Tests: gradcheck against finite differences for every op.

### Phase 2: Language surface

  constructors (trainable vs. non-trainable leaves).

  list of parameters; returns array(s) matching their shape.

  tape when any input is a tracked tensor.

# Linear softmax classifier on a 3-class gaussian-blobs dataset.
#
# This version uses MLPL's autograd: parameters are declared with
# `param[...]` and gradients are computed via the `grad` built-in.
# SGD updates stay manual (W <- W - lr * grad).
#
# Renders:
#   - confusion matrix of predicted vs true labels
#   - the class-0 decision boundary on a 2D grid

# ----- data -----
D  = blobs(11, 30, [[0, 0], [4, 4], [-4, 4]])
X  = matmul(D, [[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]])
tl = reshape(matmul(D, [[0],[0],[1]]), [90])
Y  = one_hot(tl, 3)

# Precomputed broadcast helper (grad's mini-evaluator can't call
# `ones` directly, so we lift it into an env variable used as a
# const leaf inside the loss expression).
O90 = ones([90, 1])

# ----- parameters -----
# Bias has shape [1, 3] so it broadcasts via matmul(O90, b).
W  = param[2, 3]
b  = param[1, 3]
lr = 0.2

# ----- training (MSE-on-one-hot loss via grad) -----
# The loss expression uses only ops supported by the grad tape
# lifter: matmul, softmax (unary, last axis), elementwise arith,
# and mean. It is recomputed fresh for each parameter's grad call.
repeat 300 {
  W = W - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y)), W);
  b = b - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y)), b)
}

# ----- evaluation -----
logits = matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)
P      = softmax(logits, 1)
pred   = argmax(P, 1)
accuracy = mean(eq(pred, tl))
accuracy

# Confusion matrix: predicted vs true labels.
confusion_matrix(pred, tl)

# ----- decision boundary view -----
# Evaluate the classifier on a 30x30 grid spanning the data range, then
# render the class-0 probability as the surface with the original
# points overlaid (as [x, y, label] via the stored blobs matrix).
G    = grid([-6, 6, -3, 6], 30)
O900 = ones([900, 1])
gl   = matmul(G, W) + matmul(O900, b)
gP   = softmax(gl, 1)
# Extract class-0 probability via matmul projection.
p0   = reshape(matmul(gP, [[1],[0],[0]]), [900])
boundary_2d(p0, [30, 30], X, tl)

# Linear softmax classifier on a 3-class gaussian-blobs dataset.
#
# This version uses MLPL's autograd: parameters are declared with
# `param[...]` and gradients are computed via the `grad` built-in.
# SGD updates stay manual (W <- W - lr * grad).
#
# Renders:
#   - confusion matrix of predicted vs true labels
#   - the class-0 decision boundary on a 2D grid

# ----- data -----
D  = blobs(11, 30, [[0, 0], [4, 4], [-4, 4]])
X  = matmul(D, [[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]])
tl = reshape(matmul(D, [[0],[0],[1]]), [90])
Y  = one_hot(tl, 3)

# Precomputed broadcast helper (grad's mini-evaluator can't call
# `ones` directly, so we lift it into an env variable used as a
# const leaf inside the loss expression).
O90 = ones([90, 1])

# ----- parameters -----
# Bias has shape [1, 3] so it broadcasts via matmul(O90, b).
W  = param[2, 3]
b  = param[1, 3]
lr = 0.2

# ----- training (MSE-on-one-hot loss via grad) -----
# The loss expression uses only ops supported by the grad tape
# lifter: matmul, softmax (unary, last axis), elementwise arith,
# and mean. It is recomputed fresh for each parameter's grad call.
repeat 300 {
  W = W - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y)), W);
  b = b - lr * grad(mean((softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y) * (softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)) - Y)), b)
}

# ----- evaluation -----
logits = matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)
P      = softmax(logits, 1)
pred   = argmax(P, 1)
accuracy = mean(eq(pred, tl))
accuracy

# Confusion matrix: predicted vs true labels.
confusion_matrix(pred, tl)

# ----- decision boundary view -----
# Evaluate the classifier on a 30x30 grid spanning the data range, then
# render the class-0 probability as the surface with the original
# points overlaid (as [x, y, label] via the stored blobs matrix).
G    = grid([-6, 6, -3, 6], 30)
O900 = ones([900, 1])
gl   = matmul(G, W) + matmul(O900, b)
gP   = softmax(gl, 1)
# Extract class-0 probability via matmul projection.
p0   = reshape(matmul(gP, [[1],[0],[0]]), [900])
boundary_2d(p0, [30, 30], X, tl)

# Linear softmax classifier on a 3-class gaussian-blobs dataset.
#
# This version uses MLPL's autograd: parameters are declared with
# `param[...]` and gradients are computed via the `grad` built-in.
# SGD updates stay manual (W <- W - lr * grad).
#
# Renders:
#   - confusion matrix of predicted vs true labels
#   - the class-0 decision boundary on a 2D grid

# ----- data -----
D  = blobs(11, 30, [[0, 0], [4, 4], [-4, 4]])
X  = matmul(D, [[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]])
tl = reshape(matmul(D, [[0],[0],[1]]), [90])
Y  = one_hot(tl, 3)

# Precomputed broadcast helper (grad's mini-evaluator can't call
# `ones` directly, so we lift it into an env variable used as a
# const leaf inside the loss expression).
O90 = ones([90, 1])

# ----- parameters -----
# Bias has shape [1, 3] so it broadcasts via matmul(O90, b).
W  = param[2, 3]
b  = param[1, 3]
lr = 5.0

# ----- training (cross-entropy loss via grad) -----
# Loss = -(1/N) * sum(Y * log(softmax(logits))). The grad tape
# lifter supports matmul, softmax (unary, last axis), log, sum,
# and elementwise arith, so this composes cleanly. Loss is
# recomputed fresh for each parameter's grad call.
repeat 1000 {
  W = W - lr * grad(0 - sum(Y * log(softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)))) / 90, W);
  b = b - lr * grad(0 - sum(Y * log(softmax(matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)))) / 90, b)
}

# ----- evaluation -----
logits = matmul(X, W) + matmul(O90, b)
P      = softmax(logits, 1)
pred   = argmax(P, 1)
accuracy = mean(eq(pred, tl))
accuracy

# Confusion matrix: predicted vs true labels.
confusion_matrix(pred, tl)

# ----- decision boundary view -----
# Evaluate the classifier on a 30x30 grid spanning the data range, then
# render the class-0 probability as the surface with the original
# points overlaid (as [x, y, label] via the stored blobs matrix).
G    = grid([-6, 6, -3, 6], 30)
O900 = ones([900, 1])
gl   = matmul(G, W) + matmul(O900, b)
gP   = softmax(gl, 1)
# Extract class-0 probability via matmul projection.
p0   = reshape(matmul(gP, [[1],[0],[0]]), [900])
boundary_2d(p0, [30, 30], X, tl)

---
name: mlpl-repl script mode
description: Use `mlpl-repl -f <file>` for .mlpl scripts; piping via stdin breaks multi-line blocks
type: reference
---

To run a `.mlpl` script end-to-end, use `cargo run -p mlpl-repl --quiet -- -f path/to/script.mlpl`.

**Why:** The interactive REPL loop reads line-by-line, so `cat file.mlpl | mlpl-repl` treats each line as a separate `eval_line` call. Multi-line constructs like `repeat N { ... }` get split across lines and the body statements execute outside the loop, producing silently wrong results (e.g. 87% accuracy instead of 100%).

**How to apply:** Always use `-f` when verifying demo files or any script containing `repeat { ... }` or other multi-line blocks. Only the final expression's value is printed in script mode — insert a bare identifier on the last line if you need to see a specific value.

=== PLAN ===
# MLPL Autograd v1 Saga (v0.5)

## Goal

Make differentiation a language primitive. Land a reverse-mode
autograd engine on the existing `DenseArray` substrate, expose
`tensor[...]`, `param[...]`, and `grad` at the language level,
and port the v0.4 demos that currently hand-write their
gradients onto the new engine. No new backends, no optimizers
beyond SGD, no model DSL -- those are later sagas.

## Quality requirements (every step)

1. TDD: failing tests first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test` (all tests)
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-array` DenseArray, shape, reductions, matmul
- `mlpl-runtime` built-ins (softmax, argmax, blobs, random, ...)
- `mlpl-eval` tree-walking evaluator
- `mlpl-parser` with current surface syntax
- v0.4 demos: `tiny_mlp.mlpl`, `softmax_classifier.mlpl`, ...
  (both hand-rolling gradient formulas)

## Phases

### Phase 1: Autograd core (Rust)
- New crate `mlpl-autograd` with a reverse-mode tape.
- `Tensor` wrapper holding `{ value: DenseArray, grad: RefCell<Option<DenseArray>>, node: NodeId }`.
- Graph nodes for: add, sub, mul, div, neg, matmul, sum,
  mean, exp, log, relu, tanh, sigmoid, softmax, transpose,
  broadcast, reshape.
- `backward(root)` walks the tape in reverse topo order and
  accumulates gradients into leaf tensors marked `requires_grad`.
- Tests: gradcheck against finite differences for every op.

### Phase 2: Language surface
- Parser support for `param[shape]` and `tensor[shape]`
  constructors (trainable vs. non-trainable leaves).
- New built-in `grad(expr, wrt)` where `wrt` is a parameter or
  list of parameters; returns array(s) matching their shape.
- Evaluator wires array-valued operations through the autograd
  tape when any input is a tracked tensor.
- Tests: end-to-end `grad` calls on scalar and vector losses.

### Phase 3: Demo ports
- Rewrite `demos/tiny_mlp.mlpl` to use `param` + `grad` instead
  of hand-written backprop. Must still pass the existing
  "MLP beats linear on XOR" integration test.
- Rewrite `demos/softmax_classifier.mlpl` the same way. Must
  still reach > 95% accuracy on separable blobs.
- Keep SGD manual for now (`W <- W - lr * grad`).

### Phase 4: Tutorial, docs, release
- Add tutorial lesson "Automatic differentiation" walking from
  a scalar example to the MLP port.
- Update `docs/milestone-ml.md` (or new `milestone-autograd.md`),
  `docs/saga.md`, `docs/status.md`, `docs/plan.md`.
- Bump REPL banner to v0.5.
- Rebuild and deploy `pages/`.
- Tag `v0.5.0-autograd`.

## Success criteria

- `mlpl-autograd` crate exists with gradcheck-verified ops.
- `grad` built-in works in the REPL for scalar and vector losses.
- Tiny MLP and softmax classifier demos pass their existing
  accuracy tests with zero hand-written gradient math.
- Tutorial lesson on autograd renders in the web REPL.
- All quality gates green, pages deployed, release tagged.

# MLPL Autograd v1 Saga (v0.5)

## Goal

Make differentiation a language primitive. Land a reverse-mode
autograd engine on the existing `DenseArray` substrate, expose
`tensor[...]`, `param[...]`, and `grad` at the language level,
and port the v0.4 demos that currently hand-write their
gradients onto the new engine. No new backends, no optimizers
beyond SGD, no model DSL -- those are later sagas.

## Quality requirements (every step)

1. TDD: failing tests first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test` (all tests)
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-array` DenseArray, shape, reductions, matmul
- `mlpl-runtime` built-ins (softmax, argmax, blobs, random, ...)
- `mlpl-eval` tree-walking evaluator
- `mlpl-parser` with current surface syntax
- v0.4 demos: `tiny_mlp.mlpl`, `softmax_classifier.mlpl`, ...
  (both hand-rolling gradient formulas)

## Phases

### Phase 1: Autograd core (Rust)
- New crate `mlpl-autograd` with a reverse-mode tape.
- `Tensor` wrapper holding `{ value: DenseArray, grad: RefCell<Option<DenseArray>>, node: NodeId }`.
- Graph nodes for: add, sub, mul, div, neg, matmul, sum,
  mean, exp, log, relu, tanh, sigmoid, softmax, transpose,
  broadcast, reshape.
- `backward(root)` walks the tape in reverse topo order and
  accumulates gradients into leaf tensors marked `requires_grad`.
- Tests: gradcheck against finite differences for every op.

### Phase 2: Language surface
- Parser support for `param[shape]` and `tensor[shape]`
  constructors (trainable vs. non-trainable leaves).
- New built-in `grad(expr, wrt)` where `wrt` is a parameter or
  list of parameters; returns array(s) matching their shape.
- Evaluator wires array-valued operations through the autograd
  tape when any input is a tracked tensor.
- Tests: end-to-end `grad` calls on scalar and vector losses.

### Phase 3: Demo ports
- Rewrite `demos/tiny_mlp.mlpl` to use `param` + `grad` instead
  of hand-written backprop. Must still pass the existing
  "MLP beats linear on XOR" integration test.
- Rewrite `demos/softmax_classifier.mlpl` the same way. Must
  still reach > 95% accuracy on separable blobs.
- Keep SGD manual for now (`W <- W - lr * grad`).

### Phase 4: Tutorial, docs, release
- Add tutorial lesson "Automatic differentiation" walking from
  a scalar example to the MLP port.
- Update `docs/milestone-ml.md` (or new `milestone-autograd.md`),
  `docs/saga.md`, `docs/status.md`, `docs/plan.md`.
- Bump REPL banner to v0.5.
- Rebuild and deploy `pages/`.
- Tag `v0.5.0-autograd`.

## Success criteria

- `mlpl-autograd` crate exists with gradcheck-verified ops.
- `grad` built-in works in the REPL for scalar and vector losses.
- Tiny MLP and softmax classifier demos pass their existing
  accuracy tests with zero hand-written gradient math.
- Tutorial lesson on autograd renders in the web REPL.
- All quality gates green, pages deployed, release tagged.

3	pub fn toggle_tutorial(lesson: UseStateHandle<Option<usize>>) -> Callback<web_sys::MouseEvent> {
4	    Callback::from(move |_| {
5	        if lesson.is_some() {
6	            lesson.set(None);
7	        } else {
8	            lesson.set(Some(0));
9	        }
10	    })
11	}
12	
13	pub fn step_lesson(
14	    lesson: UseStateHandle<Option<usize>>,
15	    delta: i32,
16	) -> Callback<web_sys::MouseEvent> {
17	    Callback::from(move |_| {
18	        if let Some(cur) = *lesson {
19	            let next = i32::try_from(cur).unwrap_or(0) + delta;
20	            if next >= 0 {
21	                let next_usize = next as usize;
22	                if next_usize < LESSONS.len() {
23	                    lesson.set(Some(next_usize));
24	                }
25	            }
26	        }
27	    })
28	}
29	
30	pub fn run_example(
31	    on_submit: Callback<String>,
32	    input_value: UseStateHandle<String>,
33	) -> Callback<String> {
34	    Callback::from(move |line: String| {
35	        input_value.set(line.clone());
36	        on_submit.emit(line);
37	    })
38	}

Each row of A is a probability distribution over the keys -- a weight map for one query. The scaling by sqrt(d) keeps the scores from growing with the key dimension so softmax doesn't saturate. When K = Q (self-attention), each token's highest score is on itself, so the diagonal of A dominates. Rendering A as a heatmap shows the full attention pattern at a glance.",

283	            "Q = randn(17, [6, 4])",
284	            "K = randn(23, [6, 4])",
285	            "scores = matmul(Q, transpose(K)) / sqrt(4)",
286	            "A = softmax(scores, 1)",

288	        ],
289	        try_it: "Set K = Q and re-render the heatmap -- notice how the diagonal becomes the brightest cell in each row.",
290	    },
291	];

        intro: "MLPL has built-in autograd. Declare a trainable leaf with param[shape] and compute gradients with grad(loss_expr, wrt). intro: "Scaled dot-product attention is a single matrix expression: A = softmax(Q K^T / sqrt(d), 1). Each row of A is a probability distribution over the keys -- a weight map for one query. The scaling by sqrt(d) keeps the scores from growing with the key dimension so softmax doesn't saturate. #!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Build pages/ for GitHub Pages deployment.
# Run this before committing pages/ changes.

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_DIR="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
WEB_DIR="$PROJECT_DIR/apps/mlpl-web"

echo "=== Building pages/ ==="
cd "$WEB_DIR"
mkdir -p "$PROJECT_DIR/pages"
touch "$PROJECT_DIR/pages/.nojekyll"
trunk build --release --public-url /sw-mlpl/
rsync -a --delete --exclude='.nojekyll' "$PROJECT_DIR/dist/" "$PROJECT_DIR/pages/"

echo "=== Done ==="
echo "Pages built in: $PROJECT_DIR/pages/"
echo "To deploy: git add pages/ && git commit && git push"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Build pages/ for GitHub Pages deployment.
# Run this before committing pages/ changes.

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_DIR="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
WEB_DIR="$PROJECT_DIR/apps/mlpl-web"

echo "=== Building pages/ ==="
cd "$WEB_DIR"
mkdir -p "$PROJECT_DIR/pages"
touch "$PROJECT_DIR/pages/.nojekyll"
trunk build --release --public-url /sw-mlpl/
rsync -a --delete --exclude='.nojekyll' "$PROJECT_DIR/dist/" "$PROJECT_DIR/pages/"

echo "=== Done ==="
echo "Pages built in: $PROJECT_DIR/pages/"
echo "To deploy: git add pages/ && git commit && git push"

feat(web): add Automatic Differentiation tutorial lesson

## Saga 9: Autograd v1 (COMPLETE)
Reverse-mode autograd as a language primitive. New `mlpl-autograd`
crate provides a tape-based `Tensor` with backward over add, sub,
mul, div, neg, exp, log, relu, tanh, sigmoid, softmax, sum, mean,
transpose, reshape, and matmul, all gradcheck-verified against
finite differences. Parser surface adds `param[shape]` and
`tensor[shape]` constructors, and a new `grad(expr, wrt)` built-in
lifts array expressions onto the tape and returns the gradient
with respect to a tracked parameter. The v0.4 `tiny_mlp` and
`softmax_classifier` demos were ported to use `param + grad`
instead of hand-written backprop, and a new "Automatic
Differentiation" tutorial lesson walks from a scalar minimization
to a one-layer linear regression. Delivered v0.5.

## Saga 8: ML demos (COMPLETE)

Repo compartmentalization scaffolding.

## Saga 0: Foundation (COMPLETE)
Foundation and contracts.

## Saga 1: Dense tensor substrate v1 (COMPLETE)
Shape, DenseArray, reshape, transpose, indexing.

## Saga 2: Parser and evaluator foundation (COMPLETE)
Lexer, AST, parser with precedence, AST-walking evaluator.

Working REPL with all v1 syntax, built-in functions, tracing.

TraceEvent/Trace types, evaluator instrumentation, JSON export.

Sagas 0 through 4 complete. MVP ships with REPL + trace export.

## Saga 6: ML foundations (COMPLETE)

array constructors (zeros, ones, fill), and the first end-to-end

into the browser REPL: k-means clustering, PCA via power iteration,
a linear softmax classifier, a tiny MLP on XOR-style data, and a
scaled dot-product attention pattern. Tutorial lessons added for
each. Delivered v0.4.

40	
41	## Future saga sequence
42	
43	### Saga 9 -- Autograd v1 (NEXT)
44	Reverse-mode automatic differentiation on the existing
45	`DenseArray` substrate. Introduces a `mlpl-autograd` crate with a
46	tape-based graph, `param[...]` / `tensor[...]` constructors, and a
47	`grad` built-in. Port the tiny MLP and softmax classifier demos
48	off hand-written gradients as the acceptance test. No new
49	backends, no optimizers beyond SGD.
50	
51	Why first: every later saga (optimizers, model DSL, LoRA,
52	training loop, distillation) assumes differentiation is a
53	language primitive. Doing it now is cheap because the surface
54	area is still small; doing it later means rewriting the demos
55	twice.
56	
57	### Saga 10 -- Optimizers and training loop
58	Adam, momentum-SGD, learning-rate schedules, and a composable
59	`train ... |> ...` pipeline operator. Richer synthetic datasets
60	(moons, circles, spirals) from the v0.4 "what's next" list.
61	Live loss-curve animation via the existing trace pipeline.
62	
63	### Saga 11 -- Model DSL
64	`chain[...]`, `residual[...]`, `norm[...]`, `attention[...]`
65	combinators. Parameter trees with named addressing. Rebuild the
66	tiny MLP and attention demos using the DSL; add a 2-layer
67	transformer block demo (still CPU, still tiny).
68	
69	### Saga 12 -- Tokenizers, datasets, and experiment tracking
70	Streaming/lazy dataset ops (`load`, `tokenize`, `shuffle`,
71	`batch`), a byte-level BPE tokenizer, and `experiment "name"`
72	objects with seed control, config snapshots, and logged metrics.
73	Reproducibility story lands here.
74	
75	### Saga 13 -- Tiny LM end-to-end
76	Train a character-level or tiny-BPE language model (~1-5M params)
77	on a small corpus entirely in MLPL on CPU. Visualize loss,
78	sample generations, and attention maps. This is the first saga
79	that proves the platform thesis end-to-end.
80	
81	### Saga 14 -- MLX backend
82	Backend abstraction layer (`backend <- mlx | cpu`), lazy
83	execution graph, kernel fusion for the hot ops. Re-run the tiny
84	LM on Apple Silicon and show a concrete speedup. CUDA deferred.
85	
86	### Saga 15 -- LoRA / QLoRA and quantization
87	`lora[rank=8] model`, layer-scoped adapters, shared subspaces,
88	4-bit quantization for the frozen base. Fine-tune the Saga 13
89	model on a small instruction set.
90	
91	### Saga 16 -- Embedding visualization and RAG
92	`embed`, `plot pca/tsne/umap`, 3D scatter via SVG projection,
93	nearest-neighbor links. RAG pipeline demo over a small corpus.
94	Builds directly on the existing viz stack.
95	
96	### Saga 17 -- CUDA backend and distributed execution
97	CUDA kernels for the fused ops from Saga 14; `run model on
98	nodes[...]` primitives; device placement syntax. Homelab LAN
99	training demo.
100	
101	### Saga 18 -- Distillation, ICL/ICRL, engram memory
102	Distillation pipelines, trajectory-based updates, engram-style
103	memory attachments, and multi-model orchestration
104	(`orchestrate[planner, executor, verifier]`). The research
105	frontier items from section 6 of `research2.txt`.
106	
107	### Saga 19 -- LLM-as-tool integration
108	REST client built-ins, teacher-model distillation workflows,
109	codegen helpers. Intentionally last: secondary to the
110	"build your own model" story.
111	
112	## Dependency graph (abbreviated)
113	
114	```
115	9 autograd
116	  |-- 10 optimizers -- 11 model DSL -- 12 data+tracking -- 13 tiny LM
117	  |                                                            |

  adjust. Visualization is core syntax, not an afterthought.

# ML Foundations Milestone (v0.2.0)

Building on the MVP, this milestone adds matrix multiplication, math
functions, and enough infrastructure to train a logistic regression model.

## Delivered

- [x] Vector dot product (`dot`)
- [x] Matrix multiplication (`matmul`)
- [x] Element-wise math functions (`exp`, `log`, `sqrt`, `abs`)
- [x] Power function (`pow`)
- [x] ML activations (`sigmoid`, `tanh_fn`)
- [x] Comparison operators (`gt`, `lt`, `eq`)
- [x] Statistical functions (`mean`)
- [x] Array constructors (`zeros`, `ones`, `fill`)
- [x] Loop construct (`repeat N { ... }`)
- [x] Logistic regression training demo (AND gate)
- [x] Updated REPL `:help` with all 23 built-in functions
- [x] Updated README with full function reference

## Built-in Function Count

- MVP (v0.1): 9 functions
- ML milestone (v0.2): 23 functions (+14)

## Demo: Logistic Regression

`demos/logistic_regression.mlpl` trains a 2-parameter model to learn the
AND gate using 300 steps of gradient descent. The model converges to 100%
accuracy on the 4-sample dataset.

# Autograd Milestone (v0.5.0)

This milestone makes differentiation a language primitive. A new
`mlpl-autograd` crate provides a reverse-mode tape on top of the
existing `DenseArray` substrate, the parser learns `param[shape]`
and `tensor[shape]` constructors, and a new `grad(expr, wrt)`
built-in lifts array expressions onto the tape and returns the
gradient with respect to a tracked parameter.

## Delivered

- [x] `mlpl-autograd` crate with reverse-mode `Tape` and `Tensor`
- [x] Tape ops: add, sub, mul, div, neg, exp, log, relu, tanh,
      sigmoid, softmax, sum, mean, transpose, reshape, matmul
- [x] Gradcheck tests against finite differences for every op
- [x] Parser surface for `param[shape]` and `tensor[shape]`
- [x] `grad(expr, wrt)` built-in via a tape-lifting mini-evaluator

  `DenseArray` substrate
